A_intro:
The immense to-do lists of educators have made teaching one of the most stressful careers (Smith
& Smalley, 2018). School-based agricultural education (SBAE) teachers, specifically, must be
knowledgeable about agriculture, food, and natural resources (Easterly & Simpson, 2020), manage an FFA
chapter (Mundt & Connors, 1999), and meet the needs of the community (Traini et al., 2021). Due to these
expectations, teachers must complete various tasks simultaneously throughout their day, which leaves them
exhausted, stressed, and with little support from society (Darling-Hammond & Bransford, 2017).

These many responsibilities underscore the importance of support, including through professional
development, as a way for SBAE teachers to sustain their practice. SBAE teachers rely on professional
development to network, reenergize, and learn how to handle the stresses within their careers (Smalley &
Smith, 2017). Participation in professional development is essential to improving a teacher's practice
(Fessler & Christensen, 1992), as well as an opportunity for teachers to attain authentic, relevant, and
effective practices that improve the value of the teacher's knowledge and, in turn, promote student learning
(Hunzicker, 2011). As an example, researchers who analyzed public schools' data found that students
improved in academics when teachers employed skills acquired through professional development
opportunities, such as collaboration and peer feedback (Wei et al., 2010). Not only does professional
development improve teachers' practice, but it also contributes to the emotional, social, and personal growth
of educators (Desimone, 2009). Professional development is also related to increased self-efficacy,
including SBAE teachers (McKim & Velez, 2017; Tschannen‐Moran & McMaster, 2009; Ulmer et al.,
2013).

1 Josie Manternach, Program Officer, Community Foundation of Greater Dubuque, josie@dbqfoundation.org, 0009-

While professional development is associated with positive outcomes for educators, scholars such
as Elmore (2005) and Fullan (2007) caution that professional development does not necessarily equate to
professional learning. They argue there is no guarantee a teacher has learned and that teachers should be
learning every day, not only at "professional development" events (Elmore, 2005; Fullan, 2007).
Additionally, professional development encompasses a variety of forms, including workshops, leadership
roles, professional reading, and observing others (Desimone, 2009; Easterly & Myers, 2019). Therefore,
we will use the terms "professional development" and "professional learning" interchangeably in this
paper. This usage aligns with the calls for SBAE teacher professional development, which are often not for
specific events but areas for teacher growth (Haddad et al., 2023).

Although professional development can take many forms, teachers are seeking alternative outlets
for teacher learning as opposed to the commonly used "one shot, sit and get workshops" (Hunzicker, 2011,
p. 1). One way for teachers around the globe to access professional learning is by using social media
platforms. The growth and accessibility of online resources encourage teachers to go online to connect,
share ideas, and expand their professional learning opportunities through social media platforms
(Prestridge, 2019). Social media applications, such as Facebook, Twitter, and Instagram, allow teachers to
connect and provide spaces to share ideas with like-minded peers who share the same educational
philosophy (Carpenter et al., 2020; Prestridge, 2019).

With the continued growth of social media and professional learning networks (PLN) in education,
Krutka et al. (2016) developed a model (Figure 1) for teachers to focus on the critical elements of
professional learning network activities. Krutka et al. defined PLNs as "uniquely personalized, complex,
systems of interactions consisting of people, resources, and digital tools that support ongoing learning and
professional growth" (p. 150). The first part of the model, engaging, recognizes how educators participate
(Krutka et al., 2016). The discovery construct represents actions wherein teachers serendipitously find
resources and ideas (Krutka et al., 2016). Teachers take part in experimenting when they try out new
strategies, while reflecting encompasses how teachers deliberate on their actions after finding and trying
new teaching ideas (Krutka et al., 2016). Finally, Krutka et al. (2016) describe sharing as when the teacher
contributes knowledge or discusses professional learning.

Figure 1

Elements of teachers’ professional learning network activities

Note. Adapted from Krutka et al. (2016).

Manternach & Claflin

Journal of Agricultural Education

jae-online.org
Utilizing resources available through social media platforms has allowed teachers to explore their
learning opportunities, and teachers are using social media specifically to learn and expand their
professional development (Prestridge, 2019; Ray et al., 2022; Vu et al., 2014). Hillman et al. (2021) noted
that social media serves as a communal platform for learning, where individuals of different backgrounds
can pursue their interests. The communal learning approach is also evident within SBAE, as Teixeira et al.
(2021) found that California agriculture teachers were using a closed Facebook group to request help and
share resources related to instruction and FFA-related events. Additionally, Ray et al. found that agriculture
teachers are using social media to learn and that, overall, they find it more valuable than school-sponsored
professional development. Knowing that SBAE teachers seek professional development online, which may
impact teacher self-efficacy, this study sought to identify the relationship between SBAE teachers' social
media use for professional purposes and self-efficacy related to instruction. While we recognize that the
needs of an agriculture teacher go beyond instruction, we limited our focus to classroom teaching as that
area was identified as being targeted by teachers on social media (Teixeira et al., 2021) and building off
previously validated instruments (Krutka et al., 2016; Ray et al., 2022).

Theoretical Framework

Due to the explorations of the relationship between SBAE teachers’ professional use of social media
and their self-confidence, self-efficacy served as the theoretical framework. Self-efficacy offers a lens for
understanding how teachers’ beliefs about their abilities are shaped through social and professional
interactions. Self-efficacy, a facet of Bandura’s (1986) social learning theory, is based on learning through
observing others in a social context (Bandura, 1986). Bandura’s theory and prior research support the idea
that when an individual observes an event and makes sense of their experience, it will shape future behaviors
(Bandura, 1986). One tenet of Bandura’s work, self-efficacy, refers to the self-perception one has to
complete a task and has been used interchangeably with self-confidence (Feltz & Öncü, 2014; Tschannen-
Moran et al., 1998). For this paper, we define teaching self-confidence as the ability to employ various
strategies that may lead to student learning (Hoy & Spero, 2005).

Building on this definition of teaching self-confidence, we examine the critical role that teacher
self-confidence plays in shaping how teachers perceive and enact instructional tasks. For instance, if an
individual’s self-efficacy is high, one is more confident in themselves and likely to attempt the task, whereas
with low self-efficacy, an individual will perceive the task as challenging (McKim et al., 2017). It is also
important to note that self-efficacy is not the same across tasks (Bandura, 1997; Hoy & Spero, 2005, as it
is a forward-looking belief focused on one’s perceived competence rather than actual skill level. We
recognize the limitation of focusing on the perception of competence rather than actual ability (Hoy &
Spero, 2005). This distinction matters, as individuals often misjudge their abilities, which can shape the
actions they take and the effort they invest in them (Hoy & Spero, 2005). Tschannen-Moran et al. (1998)
proposed that teachers’ efficacy beliefs are influenced by how they interpret the resources and challenges
within their instructional settings. Supportive input from colleagues, school leaders, and parents can serve
as a form of social persuasion (Bandura, 1997). Ultimately, fostering teacher self-efficacy is incredibly
important, as even capable educators may hesitate to act if they do not believe in their abilities or have
support.

Bandura conceived of four types of experiences that impact the development of self-efficacy or
self-confidence: mastery, vicarious, social persuasion, and emotional states (Bandura, 1986). For this study,
two of the experiences, vicarious and social persuasion, were used to approach how individuals build their
self-efficacy. Vicarious experiences involve observing others accomplish a task, while social persuasion
experiences occur when an individual receives feedback from others on their ability (Bandura, 1986;
McKim & Velez, 2017). McKim and Velez note that professional development experiences could serve as
an opportunity for vicarious and mastery experiences for educators. While the link between how social
media and connections serve as professional learning opportunities is limited, social media has become an

Manternach & Claflin

https://doi.org/10.5032/jae.v67i1.3177
Journal of Agricultural Education
 4
jae-online.org
affordable and widely accessible tool for those looking to acquire knowledge that supports their professional
growth (Ahn et al., 2022; Luo et al., 2020). Social media platforms can offer professional learning
opportunities, help users find collaborators and support networks, and create spaces for dialogue around
professional challenges (Markham et al., 2018; Ray et al., 2021; Teixeira et al). Social media platforms also
provide opportunities for teachers to engage in vicarious and mastery experiences within their networks,
mirroring the opportunities available during professional development (de Jong et al., 2016; McKim &
Velez, 2017).

Teacher self-efficacy has a positive relationship with teacher effectiveness, professional
development (Tschannen‐Moran & McMaster, 2009; Tschannen-Moran et al., 1998), and stronger levels of
career commitment (McKim et al., 2017). These outcomes underscore the importance of continuing to study
self-efficacy, despite its extensive study within education realms and SBAE. By investigating SBAE
teachers’ professional use of social media as a source of self-confidence related to teaching, this study
provides insight into how digital spaces may support the development of teacher self-efficacy.

Purpose and Research Questions

The purpose of this study was to describe the self-confidence of SBAE teachers and examine the
relationship between teacher self-confidence and professional use of social media amongst SBAE teachers.
The following objectives lead this study:
1. Describe the perceived level of teacher self-confidence of SBAE teachers.
2. Compare the perceived level of teacher self-confidence of SBAE teachers by certification
type, gender, and career phase.
3. Describe the social media engagement of SBAE teachers.
4. Compare the social media engagement of SBAE teachers by certification type, gender,
and career phase.
5. Describe the relationship between SBAE teacher social media use and self-confidence.
A_methods:
A descriptive correlational survey design was employed to investigate teachers' level of selfconfidence in relation to their use of social media for professional purposes. The population for this study
was a national sample of in-service SBAE teachers. We utilized a randomized sample of 78 teachers’ names
and email addresses from the National FFA Organization. The online instrument was distributed in February
and March of 2023; the invitation email to the population introduced the study, clearly stated what was
being asked of them, explained why they were chosen, who was conducting the study, and provided a link
to the questionnaire (Dillman et al., 2014). After the initial invitation, teachers who had not yet responded
to the survey were sent three reminder emails. In the end, 128 teachers responded to the study invitation
and completed the questionnaire, resulting in a 16% response rate.

The instrument included three sections: teacher self-confidence, elements of teacher engagement
(EOTE), and demographics. The self-confidence construct was adapted from the Teaching Confidence
Scale designed by Hoy and Burke-Spero (2005) with a six-point Likert-type scale from strongly disagree
to strongly agree. The original 32-question instrument was adapted and reduced to 10 questions to align
with the study's relevance and prevent respondent fatigue. The questions that were not included were
specific to particular contexts, such as teaching effectively in an urban school or teaching basic concepts of
fractions. The word agriculture was incorporated into three of the questions to focus on the specific content
area of the study. A rating of 'strongly agree' indicated the individual's confidence in their ability to complete
the task listed (e.g., locating resources for agricultural lessons, teaching their students effectively, or
facilitating class
A_results:
showed that
SBAE teachers are generally self-confident in the classroom and in agriculture content. SBAE teachers
also use social media channels for professional purposes. Overall, there was no statistically significant
relationship between the self-confidence of SBAE teachers and their social media use for professional
purposes. However, there were differences in self-confidence and social media use based on gender and
career stage. These findings may provide practitioners with insight into how social media can be
leveraged for professional learning. Future research should consider examples of social media that SBAE
teachers use for professional purposes.

Introduction

The immense to-do lists of educators have made teaching one of the most stressful careers (Smith
& Smalley, 2018). School-based agricultural education (SBAE) teachers, specifically, must be
knowledgeable about agriculture, food, and natural resources (Easterly & Simpson, 2020), manage an FFA
chapter (Mundt & Connors, 1999), and meet the needs of the community (Traini et al., 2021). Due to these
expectations, teachers must complete various tasks simultaneously throughout their day, which leaves them
exhausted, stressed, and with little support from society (Darling-Hammond & Bransford, 2017).

These many responsibilities underscore the importance of support, including through professional
development, as a way for SBAE teachers to sustain their practice. SBAE teachers rely on professional
development to network, reenergize, and learn how to handle the stresses within their careers (Smalley &
Smith, 2017). Participation in professional development is essential to improving a teacher's practice
(Fessler & Christensen, 1992), as well as an opportunity for teachers to attain authentic, relevant, and
effective practices that improve the value of the teacher's knowledge and, in turn, promote student learning
(Hunzicker, 2011). As an example, researchers who analyzed public schools' data found that students
improved in academics when teachers employed skills acquired through professional development
opportunities, such as collaboration and peer feedback (Wei et al., 2010). Not only does professional
development improve teachers' practice, but it also contributes to the emotional, social, and personal growth
of educators (Desimone, 2009). Professional development is also related to increased self-efficacy,
including SBAE teachers (McKim & Velez, 2017; Tschannen‐Moran & McMaster, 2009; Ulmer et al.,
2013).

Purpose and Research Questions

The purpose of this study was to describe the self-confidence of SBAE teachers and examine the
relationship between teacher self-confidence and professional use of social media amongst SBAE teachers.
The following objectives lead this study:
1. Describe the perceived level of teacher self-confidence of SBAE teachers.
2. Compare the perceived level of teacher self-confidence of SBAE teachers by certification
type, gender, and career phase.
3. Describe the social media engagement of SBAE teachers.
4. Compare the social media engagement of SBAE teachers by certification type, gender,
and career phase.
5. Describe the relationship between SBAE teacher social media use and self-confidence.

Methods

A descriptive correlational survey design was employed to investigate teachers' level of selfconfidence in relation to their use of social media for professional purposes. The population for this study
was a national sample of in-service SBAE teachers. We utilized a randomized sample of 78 teachers’ names
and email addresses from the National FFA Organization. The online instrument was distributed in February
and March of 2023; the invitation email to the population introduced the study, clearly stated what was
being asked of them, explained why they were chosen, who was conducting the study, and provided a link
to the questionnaire (Dillman et al., 2014). After the initial invitation, teachers who had not yet responded
to the survey were sent three reminder emails. In the end, 128 teachers responded to the study invitation
and completed the questionnaire, resulting in a 16% response rate.

The instrument included three sections: teacher self-confidence, elements of teacher engagement
(EOTE), and demographics. The self-confidence construct was adapted from the Teaching Confidence
Scale designed by Hoy and Burke-Spero (2005) with a six-point Likert-type scale from strongly disagree
to strongly agree. The original 32-question instrument was adapted and reduced to 10 questions to align
with the study's relevance and prevent respondent fatigue. The questions that were not included were
specific to particular contexts, such as teaching effectively in an urban school or teaching basic concepts of
fractions. The word agriculture was incorporated into three of the questions to focus on the specific content
area of the study. A rating of 'strongly agree' indicated the individual's confidence in their ability to complete
the task listed (e.g., locating resources for agricultural lessons, teaching their students effectively, or
facilitating class discussions). It is essential to note that the Teaching Confidence Scale only measured
classroom-related tasks and was not entirely representative of all the roles of an agricultural educator. This

Manternach & Claflin

https://doi.org/10.5032/jae.v67i1.3177
Journal of Agricultural Education
 5
jae-online.org
was intentional as we were focused on their self-confidence in teaching, not in other program roles like
advising an FFA chapter.

In addition to self-confidence, the second section of the instrument focuses on participants’ social
media engagement for professional purposes. Social media use for professional purposes was defined as
utilizing a social media platform to network with other teachers and professionals to gain knowledge and
use resources to improve one’s teaching practice (Prestridge, 2019). This section asked participants to
identify the social media platforms they utilized and the elements of the teacher engagement (EOTE) scale
developed by Ray et al. (2022). The EOTE scale included five subscales based on Krutka et al.’s (2016)
elements of teachers’ professional learning network activities: sharing, engagement, discovery,
experimenting, and reflecting. Participants responded to the prompts using a six-point Likert-type scale.
The demographic section included questions about the years completed as a teacher, gender identity, the
highest level of education completed, and their pathway to teacher certification. A description of respondent
demographics is provided in Table 1.

Data were analyzed using descriptive statistics and non-parametric tests, Spearman’s rho
correlations, Mann-Whitney U tests, and Kruskal-Wallis tests due to data that violated assumptions. While
the Mann-Whitney U test, a non-parametric form of a parametric t-test, was used to determine if differences
existed between two groups, it was also used post-hoc after the Kruskal-Wallis test (a non-parametric form
of ANOVA) to determine where the difference was between groups (Field, 2009). Pearson’s correlation
coefficient (r) was used to calculate the effect size, with an r value of .10 indicating a small effect size,
an r value of .30 representing a medium effect size, and an r value of .50 representing a large effect size
(Field, 2009).

Cronbach’s alpha was used to analyze the reliability of each construct. The Teaching Confidence
scale and elements of EOTE were proven reliable by post hoc analysis. The Teaching Confidence scale had
Cronbach’s α = .92, and the subscales of EOTE were highly reliable, with a minimum Cronbach’s α = .85
amongst the five subscales. The subscales of EOTE were highly reliable: sharing had Cronbach’s α =
.93, discovery had Cronbach’s α = .85, experimenting had Cronbach’s Alpha α = .94, engaging had
Cronbach’s Alpha α = .87, and reflecting had Cronbach’s Alpha α = .87. In prior research with EOTE (Ray
et al., 2022), the engaging construct was separated into active and passive engagement; however, passive
engagement was found to have a Cronbach’s α of .47 and was therefore combined with engaging due to
reliability concerns.

For research objectives two and four to compare between demographic groups, new variables were
created for certification type and career phase. The certification type was collapsed into two categories:
traditional certification, those who indicated they were certified to teach through an agricultural education
bachelor’s or master’s degree, and non-traditional certification, if they indicated they were alternatively
certified, not certified, or “other.” For the career phase, teachers with zero to five years of teaching
experience were considered early-career, teachers in years six to 20 were mid-career, and teachers with
between 21 and 40 years of experience were late-career.

Non-response error was addressed by comparing early to late respondents. To define the late
respondents in the study, the waves were analyzed from the responses to the initial email and reminder
emails. Lindner et al. (2001) recommend the minimum number of late respondents be 30. The study did not
meet the 30 minimum; therefore, the late respondents were identified from the last two reminder emails (n
= 70), and early respondents (n = 74) were participants from the initial and first reminder emails as
recommended by Lindner et al. An independent t-test (α = 0.01) was run to compare the early and late
respondents. No significant difference between early and late respondents was found in self-confidence,
EOTE, or demographics sections. Therefore, non-response bias was not considered an issue; however, we
do recognize the potential for those who did not respond to hold different perspectives than those who did
respond (Lindner et al., 2001).

Findings
Research Objective #1
Research question one described the perceived level of teacher self-confidence in SBAE teachers.
The self-confidence construct describes perceived levels of teacher self-confidence through various
teaching skills. Self-confidence was measured on a 10-item, six-point Likert-type scale with 1 = strongly
disagree, 2 = moderately disagree, 3 = disagree more than agree, 4 = agree more than disagree, 5
= moderately agree, 6 = strongly agree. The means of the ten items included in the scale ranged from 5.27
to 5.64. Standard deviations of the ten items included in the scale ranged from .59 to .95. The complete 10item self-confidence construct had a grand mean of 5.35 (SD = .71), suggesting that participants generally
expressed moderate agreement with statements reflecting confidence in their ability to complete the
specified tasks. Table 2 displays each item's means and standard deviations.

Research Objective #2
The second research objective compared the teacher self-confidence of SBAE teachers by
certification type, gender, and career stage. Table 3 shows the means and standard deviations for each
group. The non-parametric Mann-Whitney U test compared the dichotomous groups within the certification
and gender categories. When comparing respondents who were traditionally certified to teach agriculture
(n = 73) and those who were non-traditionally certified (NTC; n = 27), there was not a statistically
significant difference in the overall SBAE teacher self-confidence (U = 1029.00, p = .735). The effect size
was calculated with the analysis indicating an r value of .034, which was interpreted as a small effect.
When comparing respondents by gender, the SBAE teacher self-confidence of females (n = 62) and males
(n = 41) was statistically significantly different (U = 930.50, p = .021), with the mean score for selfconfidence indicating males had a higher perceived SBAE teacher self-confidence than females. The effect
size was r = -0.22, which is a small effect (Field, 2009).

Late-career teachers with 21-40 years of teaching experience indicated the highest levels of SBAE
teacher self-confidence (M = 5.68), and early-career teachers exhibited the lowest levels of SBAE teacher
self-confidence (M = 5.30). Teachers across all career phases generally reported moderate confidence in
completing the specified tasks, with late-career teachers trending toward stronger agreement (see Table 4).
To compare teachers based on career stage, the non-parametric Kruskal-Wallis test was used to determine
if there was a significant statistical difference between the three groups of early-, mid-, and late-career
SBAE teachers. There was a statistically significant difference between groups, H (18.45), 2, p = <.001.
Post-hoc Mann-Whitney U tests were utilized to test for differences between two independent groups. Three
independent Mann-Whitney U tests were done between early- and mid-career teachers, early- and latecareer teachers, and mid- and late-career teachers (see Table 5). A statistically significant difference was
found between the early- and mid-career teachers (p = .002) with an effect size with a r value of -0.30,
which is a medium effect. A statistically significant difference was also found between the early- and latecareer teachers (p = <.001) with an effect size with a r value of -0.67, which is a large effect. While there
was no statistically significant difference between mid- and late-career teachers, the effect size indicated a
r value of -0.83, which is a large effect.

Research Objective #3
Research objective three sought to describe the social media engagement of participants. First,
respondents selected all the social media channels they used for professional purposes related to teachers.
Facebook was most utilized, with 69.3% of respondents indicating they used it for professional purposes.
The next most frequently used social media platforms were YouTube with 56.1% of respondents, Instagram
with 35.1% of respondents, Pinterest with 28.9% of respondents, and TikTok with 20.2% of respondents.

Second, to describe the elements of online teacher engagement (EOTE) of SBAE teachers,
respondents indicated the highest level of agreement with statements about how they used social media for
professional purposes. The discovery sub-scale of the EOTE scale had the highest level of agreement,
indicating participants agreed more than they disagreed that they were unintentionally exposed to teaching
ideas while using social media for professional purposes. The sharing sub-scale indicated participants
disagreed more than they agreed that they used social media to contribute and share resources. Descriptive
statistics for the EOTE subscales can be found in Table 7.

Journal of Agricultural Education
 10
jae-online.org
Research Objective #4
Research objective four sought to compare the social media engagement in SBAE teachers by
certification type, gender, and years teaching. Table 8 highlights the mean scores and standard deviations
for each construct of the EOTE by certification type, gender, and career stage. Across certification types,
both traditional and non-traditional teachers agreed more than they disagreed on all sub-scales, except
sharing, where they disagreed more than they agreed. The highest agreement for both groups was in the
discovering sub-scale, indicating they were unintentionally exposed to teaching ideas and resources on
social media. Likewise, male and female participants agreed more than disagreed on tasks associated with
all sub-scales except sharing. Additionally, males disagreed more than they agreed on the engaging subscale, suggesting they do not intentionally search for people and resources on social media for professional
purposes as often as females. Regarding career stage, early-career teachers agreed more than they disagreed
on most sub-scales, with moderate agreement on the discovery sub-scale, indicating they come across
valuable items on social media unintentionally. Mid-career teachers disagreed more than they agreed on
sharing and engaging, indicating they are less likely to contribute or seek out resources. Late-career teachers
disagreed more than they agreed across all sub-scales, including moderate disagreement on sharing,
reflecting the lowest overall engagement with social media for professional purposes.

Scores for the EOTE constructs of discovery H(16.55) = 2, p = <0.001, engaging H(13.55) = 2, p
= 0.001, and experimenting H(17.12) = 2, p = <.001 were significantly affected by career stage. Three
independent Mann-Whitney U tests were done between early- and mid-career teachers, early- and latecareer teachers, and mid- and late-career teachers (see Table 10). There was a statistically significant
difference found between the early- and mid-career teachers in discovering (p = .004), engaging (p = .02),
and experimenting (p = 0.001) constructs with medium effect sizes for discovering (r = -0.32) and
experimenting (r = -0.35), with a small effect size for engaging (r = -0.26). Four out of the five constructs
were statistically significant between early- and late-career teachers: discovering (p = <0.001), engaging (p
= <0.001), experimenting (p = <0.001), and sharing (0.03), with a medium effect for discovering, engaging,
and experimenting (r = -0.46) and a small effect for sharing (r = -0.27). Between mid- and late-career
teachers, only the discovering (p = 0.04) with a small effect size (r = 0.27) and the sharing (p = .04) construct
was found to have a statistically significant difference with a small effect size (r = 0.28).

Research Objective 5
Research question five described the relationship between SBAE teachers' social media use and
self-confidence. The relationship between teacher self-confidence and the EOTE was determined not
significant, with a negligible association in effect size (Field, 2009). Between the five constructs, discovery,
experimenting, and engagement shared a very weak, negative, statistically non-significant relationship with
self-confidence. Reflecting and sharing shared a very weak, positive, statistically non-significant
relationship with self-confidence. When examining correlations by demographic categories, only the earlycareer teacher group's self-confidence and sharing had a significant correlation (p = .011). Table 11 provides
the correlations of overall teacher self-confidence and EOTE.

Authors

https://doi.org/10.5032/jae.v67iX.XXXX
Journal of Agricultural Education
 13
jae-online.org
Discussion

Our findings offer insight into the ways SBAE teachers use social media for professional purposes,
specifically within instruction, while also confirming prior findings related to self-efficacy. However, the
study has several limitations. First, the low response rate inhibits the generalizability of the study's findings.
Second, self-reported data by participants may threaten the validity of the study. Third, the measure of selfconfidence was limited to instructional tasks and did not encompass the full scope of responsibilities of an
agricultural educator, such as advising FFA chapters or overseeing SAE programs, or all the ways SBAE
teachers engage with social media beyond instruction. Although these areas were outside the scope of our
study, they are essential parts of professional practice. Despite these limitations, the findings contribute
valuable insights into SBAE teachers' professional learning and suggest directions for future research and
practice.

Overall, the results indicated that SBAE teachers are generally self-confident in tasks related to
teaching (e.g., locating resources, assessing and evaluating student work, facilitating discussions, managing
classrooms, etc.), with over 50% of respondents moderately and strongly agreeing with all ten items. With
the demographics of respondents varying in years of teaching from zero to 40, this finding aligns with the
literature, which identifies teacher self-efficacy as stable among agricultural educators at different career
stages in agriculture education (McKim & Velez, 2015). There was no significant difference between
certification groups, which aligns with previous research comparing the self-efficacy of traditionally and
non-traditionally certified SBAE teachers (Rocca & Washburn, 2006; Swafford & Friedel, 2010). However,
males had a statistically higher perceived self-confidence than females, noted in prior literature with mixed
findings related to self-efficacy and gender. Some studies indicate similar self-efficacy levels for male and
female teachers (Malmberg et al., 2014; Rodriguez, 1997; Wolf, 2008), while others note significant
differences among genders (Fackler & Malmberg, 2016; McKim et al., 2017; Ross et al., 1996; Sak, 2015).
Additionally, the later the career phase of the educator, the higher the self-confidence score.

The trend of self-confidence related to the career phase of the teacher connects to Bandura's (1986)
work and previous literature in SBAE. Mastery experiences, recognized as one of the most influential
predictors of teacher self-efficacy (Britner & Pajares, 2006), are related to successfully completing a task,
often tied to previous successes (Bandura, 1986; McKim et al., 2017). A lack of self-efficacy experiences
will equate to a lower level of self-efficacy (Burris et al., 2010; McKim et al., 2017), which is expected in
early-career teachers. While these findings may suggest that teacher preparation programs are lacking in
developing self-confidence in preservice teachers, they instead reflect the fact that early-career teachers do
not have the same amount of mastery experiences as mid- and late-career teachers to increase their selfconfidence at the beginning of their careers.

When considering social media use amongst the participants, 92.1% utilized a variety of social
media channels within their work. Facebook and YouTube were the most used; only nine respondents
indicated they do not use social media for professional use. Other social media applications used by
participants for professional purposes included Instagram, Pinterest, TikTok, Twitter, LinkedIn, and
Snapchat. Previous research in SBAE has only explored the use of Facebook by educators (Teixeira et al.,
2021).

Participants indicated the highest level of agreement with the discovery subscale, reflecting that
they had found teaching resources on social media serendipitously. The sharing subscale had the lowest
level of agreement from participants, which could reflect discomfort among participants in sharing feelings
and opinions online. These findings echo those of Ray et al. (2022) with California SBAE teachers,
indicating the lowest agreement in the sharing construct and the highest agreement with the discovering
and experimenting constructs.

Authors

https://doi.org/10.5032/jae.v67iX.XXXX
Journal of Agricultural Education
 14
jae-online.org
There were no significant differences among the EOTE subscales based on certification types. When
examining gender, female SBAE teachers showed statistically significantly higher agreement in the
engaging and experimenting constructs, indicating a preference for participating in social media and trying
out new strategies they found through social media sources. Significant differences were found among
career phases in the discovery, engaging, and experimenting constructs. Early-career teachers showed high
agreement within these subscales compared to late-career teachers and within discovery and experimenting
only with mid-career teachers. The difference among career phases connects to previous research by Ray
et al. (2022), which indicated that the more years of teaching experience, the less SBAE teachers' perceived
usefulness was found in social media for professional uses.

Overall, using social media for professional purposes did not directly correlate with higher levels
of teacher self-confidence among SBAE teachers. However, there was a positive correlation between earlycareer teachers' self-confidence and the sharing construct, indicating that early-career teachers who
perceived themselves to have a higher self-confidence were more likely to indicate agreement with the
statements under the sharing construct. These findings may be due to the higher level of agreement across
the EOTE subscales for early-career teachers, indicating a wider use of social media for professional
purposes.

While there was no statistically significant correlation between self-confidence and the elements of
online teacher engagement, additional research is warranted based on prior findings related to self-efficacy
and professional development (McKim & Velez, 2017; Ulmer et al., 2013). Additionally, the higher levels
of agreement among early-career teachers for the EOTE subscales indicate a broader use of social media
for professional practices, as well as the positive correlations between early-career teachers' self-confidence
and their sharing of professional learning on social media.

Although no correlation was found between self-confidence and professional learning via social
media, this does not imply that teachers who use social media are not being positively impacted in other
ways. It is recognized that teachers are not limited to gaining resources and new knowledge only through
formal professional development, with social media as an emerging tool (Prestridge, 2019; Vu et al., 2014).
While still in its infancy, social media-based professional learning is occurring and demonstrates potential
to support educators, warranting further examination. Future research should investigate the motivations of
SBAE teachers for using social media and explore the broader impact on teachers and their programs. In
practice, we recommend considering social media as an area for professional learning, especially for earlycareer teachers, as we seek to strengthen support for SBAE teachers.
